title: Pasta aglio e olio
subtitle: Spaghetti with garlic, chilli, and good olive oil.
excerpt: Spaghetti tossed with garlic fried slowly in olive oil until golden, dried chilli, and a handful of parsley. Done in the time it takes to boil the pasta. The great Neapolitan midnight meal: five ingredients, nothing else.
sourceNotes: Pasta aglio e olio is one of the oldest pasta preparations in the Neapolitan tradition, predating the tomato in Italian cooking. It is listed as a cucina povera dish: the food of people who had pasta, olive oil, and garlic and nothing else. The modern version is unchanged in structure from the historical one, which is uncommon in any culinary tradition. It is associated with Naples and eaten across southern Italy as a late-night meal or a test of pasta fundamentals.

content.text: The garlic must be cooked slowly over a low heat. High heat burns it to bitter before it softens. Golden and fragrant takes about 4 minutes of patient cooking. Burnt garlic cannot be salvaged.
The pasta water is the sauce. Reserve a full mug before draining: the starchy water emulsifies with the oil and garlic to create a glossy coating rather than a greasy puddle.
A large saucepan for the pasta and a frying pan for the garlic.
Cook the pasta and the garlic

Bring a large pan of well-salted water to the boil. Cook {{pasta-dried}} of spaghetti according to the packet, aiming for al dente. While the pasta cooks, warm {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a frying pan over a low heat. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic and cook gently for 3–4 minutes until just turning golden. Add {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli flakes and cook for another 30 seconds. Remove from the heat.

Reserve a mug of pasta cooking water before draining. Drain the pasta and add it to the frying pan. Return to a medium heat and toss vigorously, adding pasta water a few tablespoons at a time until the sauce is glossy and coats the pasta. Add most of the parsley and toss again. Taste and add salt if needed. Serve immediately with the remaining parsley on top.

items.cause: Heat too high, or cooked too long.
items.fix: Discard and start again. Burnt garlic cannot be fixed. A clean pan and 3 minutes on low heat is the only path.

items.symptom: Pasta is greasy rather than glossy
items.cause: Pasta water not added, or not added while hot.
items.fix: The starchy water is essential for emulsification. Add it while the pasta is hot and toss energetically.

items.symptom: Pasta is sticking together after draining
items.fix: Drain directly into the sauce pan or add a ladle of pasta water immediately after draining to prevent sticking.

items.cause: Pasta water under-salted, or not enough garlic oil.
items.fix: Pasta water should taste like the sea. The garlic oil quantity is not excessive for this dish — it is the sauce.

content.text: Aglio e olio is the midnight meal of Naples: the dish made when you come home late and find pasta, garlic, and a bottle of oil. Its importance in Italian culinary identity is outsized relative to its simplicity, because it demonstrates that good cooking does not require many ingredients, only good technique and good oil. The British equivalent (cheese on toast, beans on toast) operates on the same principle. Both are foundational preparations that reveal as much about culinary culture as any more elaborate dish.
